learning_outcomes.description: Identify a valid research topic and devise strategies for addressing it;

Conduct independent research into the chosen topic, utilising the various resources within the University Library system, and elsewhere, as necessary.  This would naturally include relevant databases, periodical literature, collected editions, and/or a variety of other possible materials and resources;

Identify relevant information (fact, ideas, findings, etc.) in the exisiting literature and research around your topic by means of which it might be informed; structure these findings into an argument leading to valid research questions or hypotheses;
Identify and put into effect a suitable research method (or methods), within the methodological framework most applicable to the topic, in order to support your analysis or critique and findings;
Communicate the results of your research (findings, argument, conclusion) in writing, at a high standard of academic English expression;
Maintain a high standard of self critique in terms of the quality/originally of your ideas; quality and accuracy of your written expression; standards of your scholarly practice;

Demonstrate a conscientious approach to all aspects of scholarly practice, including fairness and transparency, referencing and citation, intellectual honesty, discipline-specific conduct and practices.
Observe proper ethical procedures for any aspects of the research that involve the participation of human subjects.

assessments.due_date:
7/08/2026 5:00 pm

This assessment item may be submitted any time prior to the due date.
assessments.task_description:
The project outline is to focus your thinking about your research project early in the Semester.

Length: approx.500–1000 words (excluding references).

Your project outline should be discussed with your advisor prior to submission.* The course co-ordinator will review your outline and consult with your advisor before final approval. If any further requirements are identified, these will be explained clearly and you will have time to respond.

Your project outline must:

  1. Set out the topic, scope and objectives of your proposed research.
  2. Identify the relevant methods and approaches that you will take/
  3. Provide a provisional time-time for the milestones in your project—e.g. research goals, writing goals, and so on.
  4. Provide evidence of ethics approval or application underway (if relevant).
  5. List of sources referred to in the outline.


This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.


*The Initial Project Outline may be an update and/or enlargement of your research proposal provided as part of the requirements for acceptance into the course.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submitted via TurnItIn and also emailed to your advisor.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: Evidence of Human Research Ethics Approval
assessments.due_date:
4/09/2026 5:00 pm

If obtaining the approval is likely to be delayed, please contact the course coordinator as soon as possible before the due date to discuss options that might be available for extension or changing the the proposed research design.
assessments.task_description:
For projects that require research involving human subjects or human-related materials, it is necessary to obtain the appropriate approval to conduct this research before you commence data collection. 

Research involving human subjects or human-related materials that is not accompanied by the proper approval cannot be accepted for assessment in this course. Moreover, the collection of data from human subjects or using human-materials without such approval is regarded as a form of research misconduct under the University's Policies & Procedures (see: http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/4.20.05-research-misconduct).

The latest date that evidence of approval can be provided is one week before the Semester Two census date. If you do not have ethics approval by this date, you must either:

  1. modify your research project such that it can be completed without reliance on research involving human subjects or human-related materials, or
  2. drop the course.

Please be aware that dropping the course after 31 August means you are financially liable for fees for this course.

Please note that applications for ethics approval are submitted by the supervisor as CI, and that the student must work with the advisor through the My Research portal: https://my-research.research.uq.edu.au/

For more information on obtaining ethics approval for conducting human-related research, see: https://music.uq.edu.au/student-support/research-ethics-approval-honours-and-coursework-masters.


This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Email the Course Coordinator to update them fully on the advancement of your ethics application. Work with your supervisor on the application for ethics approval via the My Research portal: https://my-research.research.uq.edu.au/

assessments.task_description:
All students are required to submit a mid-year check-in, in consultation with their supervisor(s). The check-in consists of a form to be fill-out by both the student and the advisor(s), and may be accompanied by provision of work completed by the time of check in.

The form is available on the MUSC7001 Blackboard site, and also emailed to all students ahead of the due date.

A location for submitting any relevant work is also provided on the MUSC7001 Blackboard site
assessments.submission_guidelines: Students will send their completed check-in form to their supervisor(s) in time for the supervisor(s) to complete and return to the course coordinator by the due date.

assessments.due_date:
12/04/2027 - 16/04/2027

The exact scheduling of the presentations will be advised early in the second semester of the course (i.e. semester one)
assessments.task_description:
Students will present on their research project, detailing work completed as they approach the final stages. These presentations will last 15-20 minutes with 5-10 minutes for questions. They will be scheduled during the second semester, provisionally around weeks 6–7 but subject to alteration as the precise scheduling of other events during semester becomes more apparent. All students are required to attend their colleagues' presentations.

The presentation should include overall introduction about the project in brief, as well as work completed, anticipated findings, and so on. The presentation should be prepared in consultation with supervisors.

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Students will deliver their presentations in person at the scheduled time.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
Part A (written submission) 28/05/2027 11:59 pm

Part B (oral examination) 16/06/2027

Part B oral examinations will take place no later than Wednesday in the second week of the examination period.
assessments.task_description:
The thesis submission is in two parts:

Part A: Submission of written and other materials

The thesis is c. 16,000 words in length, or, for theses with a creative component, an agreed balance of written exegesis and creative work (e.g. 8,000-word exegesis and performance of a major work).

The topic will be agreed upon primarily between the student and the advisor, although the advice of the course co-ordinator may also be sought in this matter. Students are strongly urged to follow carefully the advice of their supervisor(s) (and course co-ordinator, if applicable) in relation to the matter of topic formulation. Outcomes are much less likely to be satisfactory in cases where the student's topic gives cause for reservation with the advisor.

For matters relating to presentation, the student should:

  • follow the advice of their supervisors;
  • consult the School of Music Academic Writing Guide
  • review previous theses that are recommended by their supervisor(s) as exemplary.

It is recommended that students present a complete draft (or as-near-as-possible complete draft) of there work to their supervisor(s) at least three weeks prior to the submission deadline. It is not reasonable to expect supervisors to have time, otherwise, to provide meaningful feedback nor for students to act on this feedback in less time.

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.

Part B: Oral examination

After initial assessment of submitted materials, students will sit an oral examination in which they will be asked to discuss their work
assessments.submission_guidelines: The thesis will be submitted via the designated Turnitin link in the MUSC7001 Blackboard site. An additional upload link for non-text elements that are part of the project will be provided also in Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom O-Week (first semester) To Week 26
learning_activities.topic:
Supervision

One-to-one supervision with your appointed advisor. This will be an ongoing arrangement between student and advisor.

Students can access 12 hours of advisory meetings, distributed regularly throughout the academic year. Students typically elect for weekly meetings of 30 minutes, although individual students and their supervisors may opt for 1 hour per fortnight by arrangement.

Advisors expect demonstrable progress at supervision meetings and for students to maintain regular contact.

Students are expected to submit any draft written materials for discussion at advisory meetings at least two business days prior to the meeting. This ensures adequate time for the advisor to engage with the work. Late submission or no submission of work will reduce the usefulness and effectiveness of advisory meetings.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08
